areas.label: Foundations
areas.blurb: The framing every harness engineer needs before the rest of the map clicks. Agent = Model + Harness, the engineer-the-mistake-out rule, and the durable-vs-current distinction that survives this field's monthly churn.

areas.label: The Agent Loop & Control Flow
areas.blurb: The mechanics of think → act → observe → repeat, and the patterns that make the loop reliable instead of meandering.

areas.label: Tools & Tool Layers
areas.blurb: How the agent reaches the world: tool design, schemas, the MCP layer, error handling, selection at scale, composition, sandboxing, and the new computer-use surface.

areas.label: Context Engineering
areas.blurb: Karpathy's term for filling the context window with the right information for the next step. Window budgeting, structured prompts, compaction, position bias, long-context pitfalls, and the poisoning surface.

areas.label: Memory & State
areas.blurb: What the agent remembers across turns and sessions. Working vs long-term, episodic vs semantic, state persistence, the knowledge-base pattern, vector memory, forgetting policies, and shared memory across agents.

areas.label: Retrieval Integration
areas.blurb: Retrieval from the harness-layer perspective — not RAG 101. How RAG plugs into the agent loop, embeddings, hybrid search, query rewriting, citations, and agentic / iterative retrieval.

areas.label: Multi-Agent Orchestration
areas.blurb: When one agent isn't enough — and the more important question of when one is. Single vs multi, supervisor/worker, handoffs, routing, parallelism, debate, and the anti-patterns that have cost the field the most.

areas.label: Evals & Observability
areas.blurb: How you know whether the agent is working. Task evals, trajectory evals, LLM-as-judge, regression discipline, tracing, the current observability tools, and the cost/latency dashboards that surface problems before users do.

areas.label: Failure Modes & Reliability
areas.blurb: The discipline that distinguishes a demo from production. A failure taxonomy, structured error returns, retries/timeouts, structured output enforcement, validation gates, fallbacks, and chaos testing.

areas.label: Cost & Latency Engineering
areas.blurb: Making the agent affordable to run, and fast enough to be useful. Cost tracking, prompt caching, model tiering, parallelism, streaming, and the discipline of short context over long.

areas.label: Security & the Agent Threat Surface
areas.blurb: The new attack surface that agents create. Prompt injection, capability gating, data exfiltration, confidentiality, tool-output poisoning, audit trails, and red-teaming as a continuous practice.

areas.label: Patterns & Anti-Patterns
areas.blurb: Concrete, named patterns and anti-patterns. The shareable-on-its-own area — the kind of content readers send to teammates. Skeleton with flex, guarded tools, context recipes, eval-driven loops, the named anti-patterns, and the recipe templates for coding and research agents.

nodes.label: What is a harness?
nodes.summary: "Agent = Model + Harness." The harness is everything around the model: state, security, orchestration, memory, observability, evals. Fixing the harness often beats switching the model.
nodes.competent_means: Can name the six harness components and explain why teams routinely ship better systems on a worse model by improving the harness.

resources.title: Harness engineering for coding agent users
resources.author: Birgitta Böckeler
resources.why: The canonical vocabulary for the discipline — guides, sensors, harnessability, behaviour vs maintainability harness. Read this first; everything since builds on it.

resources.title: The Agent Harness: Why the LLM Is the Smallest Part of Your Agent System
resources.author: MongoDB
resources.why: The six-component view (state, security, orchestration, memory, observability, evals). Useful as the working mental model when auditing an existing system.

nodes.label: Engineer the mistake out
nodes.summary: Hashimoto's rule: every time the agent makes a mistake, you engineer the harness so it physically cannot make that mistake again — instead of prompt-fixing and hoping.
nodes.competent_means: Can identify three of their own recent "fixed it with a prompt tweak" moments and re-describe what the proper harness-level fix would have been.

resources.title: My AI Adoption Journey
resources.author: Mitchell Hashimoto
resources.why: The essay that named the discipline. Read the section formalizing "agent = model + harness" and the "engineer the mistake out" philosophy — short, opinionated, foundational.

nodes.label: Durable concepts vs current tools
nodes.summary: The field churns monthly. Concepts (the agent loop, eval discipline, memory architecture) outlive tool names (the framework du jour, the MCP server you ship today). Learn to tell them apart.
nodes.competent_means: When reading a new framework's docs, can name which durable concept each feature implements, and can argue when a new tool is meaningful progress vs a rename.

resources.title: Don't Build Multi-Agents
resources.author: Cognition AI
resources.why: A model of separating durable principle (context shapes behavior) from the current-tool fashion (multi-agent frameworks). Read it for the framing more than the conclusion.

nodes.label: What 'competent' looks like
nodes.summary: Every node in this roadmap carries a behavioural rubric — what being competent at this thing actually looks like — instead of "understands X." Use the rubrics to self-score honestly.
nodes.competent_means: Can score themselves on at least three recent nodes and identify where they would actually fail the rubric in production.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Frames agent competence behaviorally — what a working system does, not what it knows. Pairs well with the rubric mindset this node captures.

nodes.label: The basic agent loop
nodes.summary: think → act → observe → repeat. The minimal viable agent. Most frameworks hide this; you should be able to write it yourself.
nodes.competent_means: Can implement a working agent loop in ~50 lines without a framework and name three things the framework was hiding from you.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The cleanest "build it yourself before you import a framework" guide in the field. Walks through the loop with code, not metaphors.

nodes.label: ReAct (reasoning + acting)
nodes.summary: Interleaved reasoning and tool calls. The canonical "think out loud, then act, then read what happened" pattern.
nodes.competent_means: Can explain why interleaving beats plan-then-act-then-stop, and can identify when your traces show degenerate reasoning (reasoning that doesn't change the action).

resources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
resources.author: Yao et al.
resources.why: The canonical paper. Read sections 2 and 4; skim the rest. The framing has held up; the benchmark numbers are dated.

nodes.label: Plan-and-execute
nodes.summary: Generate a plan first, execute the plan, replan on failure. Trades reactivity for explicitness and saves tokens on cheap models.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when planning pays off (long-horizon tasks, expensive tools) vs when ReAct's reactivity wins (uncertain environments, cheap tools).

resources.title: Plan-and-Solve Prompting: Improving Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought Reasoning by Large Language Models
resources.author: Wang et al.
resources.why: The grounded version of "make a plan first." Treats planning as a prompt technique, not a separate architecture — saves you from over-engineering the planner role.

nodes.label: Reflexion / self-critique
nodes.summary: The agent critiques its own output, then retries with the critique in context. Cheap reliability win when verification is cheaper than generation.
nodes.competent_means: Can implement a one-shot reflexion pass and articulate the cost — when the second model call doesn't earn its tokens.

resources.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
resources.author: Shinn et al.
resources.why: The paper that formalized the pattern. The "verbal RL" framing is the durable insight; the specific benchmarks are dated.

nodes.label: Tree-of-Thoughts & search-based loops
nodes.summary: LATS, ToT, MCTS-flavored agent search. Heavier than ReAct; sometimes worth the cost on hard, low-feedback tasks.
nodes.competent_means: Can name the cost/latency tax and articulate the small class of tasks where it actually pays off (deep reasoning, no environment feedback).

resources.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
resources.author: Yao et al.
resources.why: The seminal paper. Read it for the framing of search-over-thought rather than the benchmark gains — the gains have closed.

nodes.label: Deterministic skeletons
nodes.summary: Hard-code the deterministic backbone (state machine, DAG) and let the model fill flexible steps. The single biggest reliability lever you have.
nodes.competent_means: Can refactor an open-ended agent into a skeleton+flexible-step design and can name three steps that should never be left to the model.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "workflow vs agent" framing in this post is the clearest in the field — the case for deterministic backbones is made with worked examples.

nodes.label: Halting conditions & loop budgets
nodes.summary: Step budgets, time budgets, repeat-detection, termination conditions. Most production agent fails are halting failures, not reasoning failures.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three ways an agent fails to halt (oscillation, monotonic grind, false-success exit) and has implemented a detector for each.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "stopping criteria" guidance is the most actionable on the web — treats halting as a first-class design decision, not an afterthought.

nodes.label: Human-in-the-loop checkpoints
nodes.summary: Pause points, approval gates, ask-to-clarify. The escape hatch that keeps the agent useful when confidence is low.
nodes.competent_means: Can design a HITL gate that doesn't kill latency (asynchronous, batched approvals, well-chosen interruption points).

resources.title: Human-in-the-loop
resources.author: LangGraph
resources.why: Current-tool example (durability:current) — the clearest implementation walkthrough for a HITL gate in a real framework; the principles transfer if LangGraph rotates out of fashion.

nodes.label: Tool & function design
nodes.summary: Naming, single-purpose vs swiss-army, return shapes, descriptions as prompts. The model only succeeds at tool use if the tools are well-named and well-described.
nodes.competent_means: Can audit a tool list and remove three tools without losing capability. Knows that the tool description is part of the prompt.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The tool-design section is the single best practitioner-grade treatment; calls out the descriptions-as-prompts insight explicitly.

nodes.label: Schema design for tool I/O
nodes.summary: JSON Schema, descriptions as prompts, enums, structured outputs. Over-permissive schemas degrade tool-use accuracy by silently inviting bad inputs.
nodes.competent_means: Can show a before/after where tightening a tool schema raised accuracy without changing the model.

resources.title: JSON Schema for OpenAPI
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.why: The reference for structured-output enforcement at the model boundary — durable because schemas-as-contracts is a permanent pattern, even as specific platform APIs evolve.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.summary: The 2025 protocol for standardizing tool access across models and clients. Replaces bespoke tool-wiring with a shared interface.
nodes.competent_means: Can ship an MCP server in an afternoon and knows when MCP is overkill (one local tool, no client diversity).

resources.title: Model Context Protocol — specification
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The canonical spec. Read the overview + transports; skim the rest until you need it. Mandatory reading once you have more than one tool client.

nodes.label: Tool errors as model input
nodes.summary: Errors are signals the model can read and act on. A 400 stack trace back into the prompt is usually worse than a structured "you passed an invalid date; expected ISO-8601."
nodes.competent_means: Distinguishes "the tool errored" from "the model misused the tool" in traces, and shapes error returns so the model can recover.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "tools-as-API-surface" guidance covers error shapes explicitly — the only post that treats them as a model-input design problem, not a logging concern.

nodes.label: Tool selection at scale
nodes.summary: What happens when the agent has 50 tools — retrieval-based tool selection, tool-bench-style filtering, dynamic tool loading.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the breakpoint where tool count starts hurting accuracy (varies by model) and has a strategy for crossing it.

resources.title: ToolBench / ToolLLM
resources.author: Qin et al.
resources.why: The clearest benchmark for "what happens when you scale tool counts" — the framing has held even as models have improved on the absolute scores.

nodes.label: Tool composition & chaining
nodes.summary: Pipelines and tool-to-tool data plumbing. When the model shouldn't be involved in mechanical handoffs.
nodes.competent_means: Can decide between in-prompt chaining (model decides next tool) and in-code chaining (deterministic pipeline) for a given task.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "prompt chaining" pattern in this post is the cleanest treatment of in-code vs in-prompt composition.

nodes.label: Tool sandboxing & permission scopes
nodes.summary: Code-execution sandboxes, file-system permissions, network egress controls. Every tool you give the model is also a tool you give anyone who can prompt the model.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three escape paths (shell, network, file-system) and has closed each with a default-deny posture.

resources.title: E2B — Sandboxes for AI agents (blog index)
resources.author: E2B
resources.why: Current-tool example — running index of sandbox-design posts (microVM isolation, egress limits, ephemeral filesystems). The architectural principles outlast the specific platform.

nodes.label: Computer-use tools
nodes.summary: Browser automation, shell tools, IDE plugin surface. Pixel-level vs API-level tool use, and the failure modes specific to each.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the failure modes of pixel-level tool use (drift, layout churn, screenshot cost) vs API-level (auth, schema rot) and picks accordingly.

resources.title: Introducing computer use
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The clearest first-party explanation of pixel-level computer use, including the honest failure modes. Durable because the tradeoffs are intrinsic, not implementation details.

nodes.label: Context-window budgeting
nodes.summary: Token accounting, headroom, ceiling effects. Most "I'll just put everything in" agents are bad budgets, not bad prompts.
nodes.competent_means: Can sketch a per-turn token budget for a real agent on the back of a napkin and identify which item is the worst-offending bloater.

resources.title: Context engineering for agents
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: The clearest practitioner-grade overview of context budgeting as a first-class design problem. Vendor-flavored but the concepts transfer.

nodes.label: System-prompt design
nodes.summary: Persona, rules, output shape, and what NOT to put in the system prompt. The system prompt is a cache-key in most production setups — design it accordingly.
nodes.competent_means: Can audit a system prompt and cut 30% without behavior loss; knows what should never live in the system prompt (per-user, per-task content).

resources.title: Prompt engineering — Anthropic docs
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Current-tool example — vendor docs, but the structural advice (be specific, use examples, define output shape) is durable and one of the few that addresses system-prompt design explicitly.

nodes.label: Compaction & summarization
nodes.summary: Auto-compact, sliding windows, hierarchical summaries. Lossy by design — the discipline is knowing which signal you're allowed to lose.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three things compaction can drop without harm and three things it can never drop. Has a metric for compaction-induced regression.

resources.title: Long-Context Window Engineering Patterns
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Vendor docs, durable principles — covers compaction patterns and when they preserve vs destroy load-bearing context.

nodes.label: Relevance filtering & pruning
nodes.summary: Trim noisy tool outputs before they hit the next turn. Most context problems are too-much-noise, not too-little-signal.
nodes.competent_means: Can show a before/after where filtering tool returns raised accuracy without changing the model or the prompt.

resources.title: Context engineering for agents
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: Discusses output-filtering as a routine harness practice — the framing of "prune what the model doesn't need" is durable even as the example tools rotate.

nodes.label: Position bias & ordering
nodes.summary: Lost-in-the-middle, recency bias, primacy bias. Models attend to the start and the end of context more than the middle.
nodes.competent_means: Can demo position bias on a current model with a runnable example and design the prompt to put the load-bearing item where the model will attend.

resources.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
resources.author: Liu et al.
resources.why: The paper that named the effect. Read sections 3–4; the U-shape attention curve is the durable insight, not the specific numbers (which improve with new models).

nodes.label: Structured context (XML, tags, sections)
nodes.summary: Anthropic-style XML, OpenAI-style sections. Structure isn't cosmetic — it changes compliance with output and ordering rules.
nodes.competent_means: Can refactor a flat prompt to structured (named sections, explicit output schema) and explain the compliance gain.

resources.title: Use XML tags to structure your prompts
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Vendor docs (durability:current), but the technique itself is durable and transfers to any model — XML, JSON-fenced sections, or markdown headers all work.

nodes.label: Long-context pitfalls
nodes.summary: 1M-token windows aren't free: latency, attention dilution, cost. Long context is a tool, not a default.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when not to use long context (when retrieval would do; when attention dilution would dominate; when latency budget says no).

resources.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
resources.author: Liu et al.
resources.why: The clearest empirical evidence that long context isn't a free win. Cite this in any "why don't we just put it all in?" argument.

nodes.label: Context poisoning
nodes.summary: Bad data in context cascades. Untrusted text from tool outputs, retrieved docs, or user input can manipulate downstream reasoning.
nodes.competent_means: Can name three ways context gets poisoned (indirect injection, retrieved-doc takeover, error-loop poisoning) and the trace pattern to catch each.

resources.title: Prompt Injection: What's the worst that can happen?
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.why: The clearest non-academic walk-through of the attack surface, including the context-poisoning angle. Required reading.

nodes.label: Working memory vs long-term memory
nodes.summary: What goes in context every turn (working) vs what's recalled when needed (long-term). Different access patterns, different storage, different failure modes.
nodes.competent_means: Can draw the memory architecture of any agent they read about and identify which memory type is doing the load-bearing work.

resources.title: Agent Memory: How to Build Agents that Learn and Remember
resources.author: Letta
resources.why: The clearest taxonomy of memory types in modern agents, including the working/long-term split as a first-class design decision rather than an implementation detail.

nodes.label: Episodic vs semantic memory
nodes.summary: "What happened last Tuesday" vs "what's true." Different retrieval strategies, different freshness needs.
nodes.competent_means: Knows which their agent actually needs (often: only one) and can defend the choice against the temptation to ship both.

resources.title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
resources.author: Packer et al.
resources.why: Treats memory as a tiered storage problem (RAM/disk analogue). The taxonomy survives the specific MemGPT implementation.

nodes.label: State & session persistence
nodes.summary: Where state lives across turns: in-process, KV store, database, blob store. The durability/latency tradeoff lives here.
nodes.competent_means: Can argue the durability/latency/cost tradeoffs for their agent's state store with a specific failure case in mind.

resources.title: Building Stateful Agents
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: Current-tool example — concrete walkthrough of state stores in a real framework. The "checkpoint as durability boundary" pattern is durable even if LangGraph rotates.

nodes.label: LLM knowledge bases (Karpathy pattern)
nodes.summary: A markdown library the LLM curates, lints, and interlinks. Replaces RAG for some use cases where retrieval is the wrong primitive.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when it beats RAG (small-to-medium evolving corpora, single user) and when it's a toy (high-traffic, low-trust, large corpus).

resources.title: LLM Knowledge Bases (gist)
resources.author: Andrej Karpathy
resources.why: The originating post for the pattern. Short, specific, and the framing — markdown library as primary memory — is the durable contribution.

nodes.label: Vector-store memory
nodes.summary: Embedding-based recall over past turns or events. Eviction policies matter more than embedding choice past a certain scale.
nodes.competent_means: Can defend their chunking strategy with a concrete example of when it failed and what the failure looked like in production.

resources.title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
resources.author: Packer et al.
resources.why: The clearest paper-treatment of "embed memories, evict on pressure, summarize before eviction." The mechanics outlive the implementation.

nodes.label: Forgetting, decay, eviction
nodes.summary: When old memory becomes a liability — stale facts, outdated preferences, drifted personas. Forgetting is a design parameter, not a bug.
nodes.competent_means: Has implemented a non-trivial eviction policy (TTL + relevance + access-recency) and watched it remove the right things.

resources.title: Agent Memory: How to Build Agents that Learn and Remember
resources.author: Letta
resources.why: Treats forgetting as a design problem with specific eviction patterns. The clearest practitioner-level coverage.

nodes.label: Shared memory across agents
nodes.summary: Multi-agent memory blackboards, shared context channels, conflict resolution. A small surface that adds a large race-condition class.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the canonical race conditions (last-writer-wins overwrite, stale read, fan-out divergence) and how each one shows up in traces.

resources.title: LangGraph multi-agent workflows
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: Current-tool example — walks through shared-state design patterns in a real framework. The race-condition lessons transfer.

nodes.label: RAG as a harness component
nodes.summary: Just enough RAG to slot it into a harness — chunk, embed, retrieve, re-insert into context. Most production RAG problems are chunking and retrieval-prompt problems, not embedding choice.
nodes.competent_means: Can explain why most "bad RAG" is bad chunking or bad query, not bad embedding. Knows the three sub-systems a RAG stack is made of.

resources.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
resources.author: Lewis et al.
resources.why: The originating paper. Read the architecture section; skim the numbers. The "retrieve, then condition" framing is the durable contribution.

nodes.label: Embeddings & dense retrieval
nodes.summary: Embedding model choice, dimensionality, normalization, the cost of switching embeddings post-launch. Most teams pick once and never revisit; they should.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three embedding-choice failure modes (domain mismatch, scale mismatch, language mismatch) and can identify which is biting them.

resources.title: MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
resources.author: Muennighoff et al.
resources.why: The reference for "which embedding model and why." The leaderboard rotates; the methodology for picking is durable.

nodes.label: Hybrid search (BM25 + dense + rerank)
nodes.summary: Pure dense retrieval loses on technical terms, acronyms, and named-entity queries. BM25 + dense + a reranker is the production default.
nodes.competent_means: Ships hybrid retrieval by default. Can show a query where dense misses and BM25 catches.

resources.title: Hybrid Search Explained
resources.author: Pinecone
resources.why: Vendor-flavored but the clearest practitioner walkthrough of why hybrid wins, with worked examples on the kinds of queries dense alone fails.

nodes.label: Query rewriting & HyDE
nodes.summary: Let the model rewrite the user's query before retrieval — expansion, decomposition, hypothesis-doc generation. Cheap when retrieval matters more than generation cost.
nodes.competent_means: Can show a query where rewriting changes the retrieved set in a way that materially improves the final answer.

resources.title: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels (HyDE)
resources.author: Gao et al.
resources.why: The canonical paper on rewriting queries via hypothetical documents. The trick generalizes: model-as-query-rewriter is a durable pattern.

nodes.label: Citations, attribution, grounding
nodes.summary: The antidote to hallucinated retrievals: make the agent show its work. Without citations you cannot audit "did this answer actually come from the source?"
nodes.competent_means: Can audit an agent's citations and find one that's fabricated. Has a citation-validation gate in their eval suite.

resources.title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
resources.author: Asai et al.
resources.why: The clearest treatment of "generation that knows when to cite and when to abstain." The mechanism is durable; the specific training recipe is one of many.

nodes.label: Agentic / iterative retrieval
nodes.summary: The agent decides what to retrieve next based on what it just read. Where the agent loop and retrieval meet — and where most "deep research" agents live.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when iterative beats single-shot retrieval (large corpora, multi-hop questions) and the cost it adds (latency, token spend).

resources.title: Self-Ask with Search
resources.author: Press et al.
resources.why: The clearest paper on "decompose, retrieve, repeat" loops. The pattern survives the specific implementation.

nodes.label: Single vs multi-agent
nodes.summary: The gate question: do you actually need multiple agents? Most "multi-agent" systems are one agent split across files for organization, not capability.
nodes.competent_means: Can argue against multi-agent for a use case where it's a fad, and identify the small class where it's earned.

resources.title: Don't Build Multi-Agents
resources.author: Cognition AI
resources.why: The strongest argument-from-experience against multi-agent as the default. Required reading before you ship a supervisor.

nodes.label: Supervisor / worker patterns
nodes.summary: One agent dispatches, others execute. Useful when the dispatcher genuinely has different concerns from the executor — not just to look organized.
nodes.competent_means: Can sketch the supervisor's tool list and explain why each tool is a supervisor concern, not a worker concern.

resources.title: Multi-Agent Workflows
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: Current-tool example — the supervisor pattern walked through with code. The structural insight (dispatcher concerns vs worker concerns) is durable.

nodes.label: Handoffs & state passing
nodes.summary: What does the receiving agent actually need to know? Most multi- agent bugs are handoff bugs — re-reading the world, dropped context, conflicting state.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three handoff bugs (dropped context, redundant re-read, conflicting overwrite) and the trace pattern that surfaces each.

resources.title: OpenAI Agents SDK — Handoffs
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.why: Current-tool example — first-party docs on handoff as a first-class primitive. The framing (handoff = transferred intent + transferred context) is durable.

nodes.label: Routing & specialization
nodes.summary: Picking the right agent for the request. Often a model call, often a classifier, sometimes a hand-tuned regex.
nodes.competent_means: Can defend their routing policy (model vs classifier vs rules) against a concrete misroute example.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "routing" pattern in this post is the cleanest treatment of when a router is a model and when it should be a rule.

nodes.label: Parallelism & fan-out
nodes.summary: Running tool calls or subagents concurrently. The latency win is real; the cost and complexity wins less obvious.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the latency/cost tradeoff for a fan-out and has chosen one with eyes open (or chosen to keep it sequential and saved the complexity).

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "parallelization" pattern (sectioning vs voting) is the cleanest treatment — distinguishes "parallel for speed" from "parallel for ensemble" cleanly.

nodes.label: Debate, voting, ensembles
nodes.summary: Multiple agents critique or vote. Theatrical wins are easy; real accuracy wins are rare and benchmark-specific.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when ensemble accuracy gains are real (uncertain domains, verifier-cheap-than-generator) vs theatrical (most cases).

resources.title: Improving Factuality and Reasoning in Language Models through Multiagent Debate
resources.author: Du et al.
resources.why: The most-cited debate paper. Read the methodology; treat the gains skeptically — newer work has shown ensemble effects often dominate the "debate" framing.

nodes.label: Multi-agent anti-patterns
nodes.summary: "Two agents pretending to talk." "One supervisor that's secretly doing all the work." The most expensive failure mode in the field — multi-agent looking like progress while paying for nothing.
nodes.competent_means: Can spot a multi-agent design that should have been one agent and name the specific cost it's paying.

resources.title: Don't Build Multi-Agents
resources.author: Cognition AI
resources.why: The most thorough catalogue of multi-agent anti-patterns from a team that shipped both. Required reading.

nodes.label: Why agent evals are different
nodes.summary: Non-determinism, multi-step trajectories, partial credit, no clean labels. Most traditional ML eval intuition is wrong for agents.
nodes.competent_means: Can explain to a backend engineer why a unit-test mindset doesn't transfer, and name three properties of agent eval that need to be designed for.

resources.title: Evaluating LLM Agents: A Deep Dive
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.why: The most practitioner-grade essay on agent eval — frames it as a discipline rather than a metric. The "look at your data" framing is durable.

nodes.label: Task-level evals
nodes.summary: End-to-end task success. A rubric, a held-out set, a pass rate. The eval you need before any of the fancier ones.
nodes.competent_means: Has a written rubric for their agent's primary task and a held-out set they didn't pick from training prompts.

resources.title: A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.why: The clearest "build the eval before you build the feature" discipline in the literature. Walks through task-level eval design with worked examples.

nodes.label: Trajectory / process evals
nodes.summary: Scoring intermediate steps, not just final outputs. Catches the regression where the agent gets the right answer for the wrong reason.
nodes.competent_means: Has caught at least one regression that task-level eval missed because the agent reached the answer via a broken path.

resources.title: Tau-Bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
resources.author: Yao et al.
resources.why: The clearest example of trajectory-aware benchmarking. The design (multi-step, tool-use, partial credit) sets the template for how to think about trajectory evals.

nodes.label: LLM-as-judge
nodes.summary: Using a model to grade a model. Cheap and scalable; calibration and bias are the catches. A judge is a model — eval it.
nodes.competent_means: Knows three ways judges fail (position bias, verbosity bias, self-preference) and the human spot-check rate they use to keep the judge honest.

resources.title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
resources.author: Zheng et al.
resources.why: The paper that established LLM-as-judge as a discipline. Section 3 is required reading on the bias catalogue.

nodes.label: Regression eval & golden sets
nodes.summary: Locking in behavior. Your eval set is your test suite — every PR runs it; regressions block merges.
nodes.competent_means: Their PR pipeline blocks on agent eval regressions. Has caught a regression in CI rather than in production.

resources.title: Evaluating LLM Agents: A Deep Dive
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.why: Treats regression discipline as the single biggest reliability lever for agents. The framing transfers directly to a PR pipeline.

nodes.label: Tracing & structured logs
nodes.summary: Per-turn traces, OpenTelemetry-style spans, replayable runs. The observability surface that makes everything else debuggable.
nodes.competent_means: Can debug a failed agent run from traces alone, without having seen it happen in real time.

resources.title: OpenTelemetry GenAI Conventions
resources.author: OpenTelemetry
resources.why: The emerging standard for instrumenting agent calls. Durable even as specific vendors come and go — the spec is the layer everyone implements against.

nodes.label: Observability tools (current)
nodes.summary: Langfuse, AgentOps, LangSmith, Laminar — the current crop. durability:current because most of these names will rotate; the capabilities they offer will not.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the four capabilities they actually want (trace search, replay, eval integration, cost dashboard) and can pick a tool by capability, not by brand.

resources.title: AgentOps
resources.author: AgentOps team
resources.why: One representative current tool. Open-source, integrates with most agent frameworks. Listed as example of the category, not a recommendation against alternatives.

nodes.label: Cost & latency dashboards
nodes.summary: The "is this agent affordable to run" view. $ per task, p50/p99 latency, cache-hit rate.
nodes.competent_means: Their agent has a $/task dashboard and a p50/p99 latency dashboard — both updated on every deploy.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "measure cost and latency from day one" guidance is unusually direct — most agent posts skip this. Durable.

nodes.label: The failure taxonomy
nodes.summary: Hallucination, loop-fail, tool-misuse, context-drift, halt-fail, permission-fail. A vocabulary for naming what went wrong before you can fix it.
nodes.competent_means: Can label any agent failure with one of these categories and name the harness component that owns the fix.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "common failure modes" section is the closest thing to a canonical taxonomy in the field. Pairs well with Hashimoto's "engineer the mistake out" rule.

nodes.label: Structured error returns
nodes.summary: Errors as model-readable signals, not stack traces. A 400 with "expected ISO-8601 date" beats a raw exception every time.
nodes.competent_means: Distinguishes "raise" from "return-as-context" and has shaped at least one tool's error vocabulary so the model can recover.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The tool-design section covers error shapes as a model-input design problem — the durable framing.

nodes.label: Retries, backoff, idempotency
nodes.summary: Standard reliability discipline applied to model + tool calls. Don't retry without backoff; don't retry without idempotency thinking.
nodes.competent_means: Can defend their retry budget per call type, and knows which of their tools are safe to retry vs which need an idempotency key.

resources.title: Patterns of Distributed Systems — Idempotent Receiver
resources.author: Unmesh Joshi
resources.why: The durable engineering treatment of how retry/backoff and idempotency actually compose. The lessons predate LLMs and transfer directly to model + tool calls.

nodes.label: Timeouts & deadlines
nodes.summary: Per-call, per-step, per-task. Every long-running agent has at least one un-bounded call hiding somewhere — find it before production does.
nodes.competent_means: Knows where their longest call lives, has a deadline on it, and has a fallback for when the deadline fires.

resources.title: Latency Numbers Every Programmer Should Know
resources.author: Peter Norvig / Jeff Dean (canonicalized by Jonas Bonér)
resources.why: The reference frame for "what's an honest deadline?" — durable because it teaches you to budget time in milliseconds, not vibes.

nodes.label: Structured output enforcement
nodes.summary: JSON Schema, function calling, constrained decoding, schema-fix loops. Never parse freeform model output in production.
nodes.competent_means: Their agent never relies on regex-parsing model text. Has a schema-fix retry path for malformed output.

resources.title: Structured Outputs
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.why: Vendor docs (durability:current) — the clearest first-party guide to enforcing JSON-schema output at the API boundary. The mechanism is increasingly standard.

nodes.label: Validation gates
nodes.summary: Post-conditions on tool output, sanity checks on plans, type- level guardrails. Rejecting a "successful" output is sometimes the right move.
nodes.competent_means: Has rejected a "successful" agent run that failed a validation gate, and can name what the gate caught.

resources.title: Guardrails for Large Language Models
resources.author: Guardrails AI
resources.why: Current-tool example — concrete walkthrough of validation as a first-class harness layer. The principle (validate structurally, validate semantically, validate behaviorally) is durable.

nodes.label: Fallbacks & degraded modes
nodes.summary: What does the agent do when the model is down, the tool is unreachable, or the budget is exhausted? A documented degraded mode beats unexplained 500s.
nodes.competent_means: Their agent has a documented degraded mode that they have actually exercised (chaos-tested or observed in prod).

resources.title: Release It! (book) — Stability Patterns
resources.author: Michael T. Nygard
resources.why: Pre-LLM but the timeouts/bulkheads/circuit-breakers material transfers directly. The most durable reliability text in this area.

nodes.label: Chaos testing for agents
nodes.summary: Inject tool failures, latency spikes, context truncation, model 503s. Most agents have never been observed under adversity until production.
nodes.competent_means: Has run a chaos test (manually injected failure) that found a real bug in their agent.

resources.title: Chaos Engineering — Principles
resources.author: Principles of Chaos
resources.why: The canonical statement of the discipline. Pre-LLM but the principles (steady-state hypothesis, blast-radius, automate) transfer directly to agent chaos testing.

nodes.label: Cost & token tracking
nodes.summary: Per-task, per-user, per-feature attribution. Without it, every "cost optimization" is guesswork.
nodes.competent_means: Knows the $/task of their agent within ~10% accuracy. Can break that down by step.

resources.title: OpenTelemetry GenAI Conventions
resources.author: OpenTelemetry
resources.why: The emerging standard includes per-call token + cost attributes. Durable because the spec is the layer everyone implements against, vendor-neutral.

nodes.label: Prompt caching
nodes.summary: Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI prefix caching, KV-cache reuse. The cheapest single optimization in modern agent design — design prompts cache-first.
nodes.competent_means: Their system prompt is cache-key-stable across requests. Knows their cache-hit rate within ~5%.

resources.title: Prompt caching with Claude
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Vendor docs (durability:current). The mechanism (prefix-stable caching with TTL) is now standard across providers; the durable lesson is "design your prompt as a cache key."

nodes.label: Model tiering & routing
nodes.summary: Cheap model for simple steps, expensive model for hard. A router decides — either by a model call or by a rule.
nodes.competent_means: Can show a tier split with a quality plot — proof the cheap tier doesn't degrade outcomes for routed-cheap requests.

resources.title: RouteLLM: Learning to Route LLMs with Preference Data
resources.author: Ong et al.
resources.why: The clearest treatment of model routing as a learned policy. The framing (preference data, calibrated routing) is durable even as specific routers improve.

nodes.label: Parallelism & batching
nodes.summary: Concurrent tool calls, batched embeddings, async generation. The latency win is real; contention bugs are the tax.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when parallelism pays (independent tool calls, embedding batches) and when it doesn't (sequential dependencies, rate- limited APIs).

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "parallelization" pattern split into sectioning vs voting is the cleanest framing — separates latency wins from ensemble wins.

nodes.label: Streaming & partial outputs
nodes.summary: Streaming for perceived latency, partial-output UX, the ergonomics of "show progress without committing to it." A UX lever as much as an engineering one.
nodes.competent_means: Knows when streaming is a UX win (long generation, chat surface) vs a footgun (structured output that must be validated whole).

resources.title: How to stream completions
resources.author: OpenAI Cookbook
resources.why: Working code example, durably hosted on the official cookbook repo. The SSE / delta-event / terminal-marker pattern walks through cleanly and transfers across providers.

nodes.label: Short-context discipline
nodes.summary: A 4k well-shaped context often beats 100k everything-in. Long context is a tool, not a default. Both latency and quality often improve with less.
nodes.competent_means: Prefers short, structured context by default and can defend reaching for long context with a specific reason.

resources.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
resources.author: Liu et al.
resources.why: Empirical grounding for "less context, attended-to, beats more context, ignored." Cite this when arguing against everything-in defaults.

nodes.label: Prompt injection
nodes.summary: Direct and indirect injection — the most-shipped vulnerability in agents. Any text the model sees from any source can carry an instruction.
nodes.competent_means: Can attack their own agent in three ways (direct user input, retrieved document, tool output) and has a defense for each.

resources.title: Prompt injection: What's the worst that can happen?
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.why: The clearest non-academic walkthrough of the attack surface and the realistic threat model. Required reading; Willison's ongoing index of the topic is the de facto bibliography.

nodes.label: Capability gating & permissions
nodes.summary: What can the agent do, and on whose behalf? Least-privilege as a default, with explicit elevation when needed.
nodes.competent_means: Agent runs least-privilege by default. Can enumerate every permission it holds and the justification for each.

resources.title: The Confused Deputy (or why capabilities might have been invented)
resources.author: Norm Hardy
resources.why: The pre-LLM origin of the capability / least-privilege framing. Applies directly to agents: the agent is the confused deputy when it can act on its principal's behalf with the attacker's intent.

nodes.label: Data exfiltration
nodes.summary: Side-channel leaks via tool calls, outbound URLs, embeddings, and logs. The model doesn't need a shell to leak — it just needs a tool that can talk to the network.
nodes.competent_means: Their agent's egress is enumerable (every outbound destination logged) and rate-limited. Knows three exfiltration paths and how each was closed.

resources.title: Markdown exfiltration (tag archive)
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.why: A running index of markdown-image exfil incidents — the general pattern (any outbound URL the model can write is a side channel) keeps reappearing in new forms. The tag archive auto-stays-current as new incidents land.

nodes.label: Confidentiality & PII
nodes.summary: Redaction, secrets handling, prompt-leak hygiene. The trace pipeline is often the worst offender, not the agent itself.
nodes.competent_means: Has a redactor in the trace pipeline that they have tested against synthetic PII. Knows what their LLM provider retains.

resources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications
resources.author: OWASP
resources.why: The reference taxonomy. Read LLM02 (insecure output handling) and LLM06 (sensitive information disclosure) — durable framing that survives version bumps of the list.

nodes.label: Tool-output poisoning
nodes.summary: Adversarial content in retrieved docs or tool returns — the indirect-injection surface every retrieval-augmented agent has.
nodes.competent_means: Distinguishes trusted from untrusted tool outputs in their pipeline and treats untrusted ones as inputs to be sanitized, not instructions to be followed.

resources.title: Not what you've signed up for: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection
resources.author: Greshake et al.
resources.why: The paper that named indirect prompt injection. Section 3 on attack vectors is required reading for anyone building a retrieval-augmented agent.

nodes.label: Audit trails
nodes.summary: Who did what, when, why — provable from logs. The forensic surface that turns a security incident into a recoverable one.
nodes.competent_means: Their agent's actions are reconstructible months later from structured logs. Has run at least one drill of "given a user report, prove what the agent did."

resources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications — LLM09 Overreliance
resources.author: OWASP
resources.why: OWASP's framing of audit trails as a defense against overreliance and an enabler of incident response. Durable even as the list version-bumps.

nodes.label: Red-teaming agents
nodes.summary: Adversarial eval as continuous discipline. Treats the threat surface like a regression eval — every release runs the attack suite.
nodes.competent_means: Runs a red-team eval on their agent on a stated cadence and has a backlog of attacks generated by the team (not just imported).

resources.title: Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models
resources.author: Perez et al.
resources.why: The canonical paper on using models to generate red-team inputs. The mechanism (generate, classify, refine) is durable even as model-specific attack catalogues change.

nodes.label: Skeleton with flexible steps
nodes.summary: Deterministic backbone, model fills the gaps. The #1 reliability pattern — the single biggest leverage point most agents miss.
nodes.competent_means: Has refactored at least one open-ended agent into a skeleton design and can describe the reliability gain it produced.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: The "workflow vs agent" framing is the cleanest treatment of the pattern. The case for backbones with flex steps is worked through with examples.

nodes.label: Guarded tool pattern
nodes.summary: Every tool has a precondition and a postcondition checker. The model can call it; the harness decides whether the call's results count.
nodes.competent_means: Has shipped a tool with both a precondition (refuses bad inputs with a structured error) and a postcondition (validates the output before returning).

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.why: Treats tool design with pre/post validation as a first-class harness concern. The structural lesson is durable.

nodes.label: Context recipes (system + user + retrieved)
nodes.summary: Named slot layouts that have been shown to work. System prompt (stable, cached), retrieved context (untrusted, sanitized), user input (untrusted, sanitized), output schema (enforced).
nodes.competent_means: Can describe their agent's context layout as a recipe with named slots and trust levels. Hasn't mixed trust levels into a single slot.

resources.title: Context engineering for agents
resources.author: LangChain
resources.why: The clearest practitioner-grade taxonomy of context slots and their roles. The recipe framing is the durable contribution.

nodes.label: Eval-driven loop
nodes.summary: Every change has an eval. Every regression blocks the change. The discipline that lets you move fast without breaking the agent.
nodes.competent_means: Their PR cycle includes an eval run, and the team has aborted at least one promising change because the eval went red.

resources.title: A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.why: The case for eval-as-the-flywheel is made with worked examples. The discipline — write the eval, run it on every change — is durable.

nodes.label: Named anti-patterns
nodes.summary: The recurring failures, given names. "Prompt the bug away." "Add another agent." "More context = more correct." "Fancy retriever, broken chunking." Each with an example.
nodes.competent_means: Can name three of their own past anti-patterns from production, with what they cost and what fixed each.

resources.title: Don't Build Multi-Agents
resources.author: Cognition AI
resources.why: The canonical "this isn't progress, it's complexity" case. The named anti-patterns survive the specific framework critique.

nodes.label: Coding-agent recipes
nodes.summary: The Claude Code / Cursor / Aider class: file-edit tool, test runner, plan-mode, repo-scoped context. The most-shipped agent shape of 2026, with reusable design choices.
nodes.competent_means: Can describe the canonical coding-agent loop (read → plan → edit → run → check) and the harness pieces that make it work (read-only tools, sandboxed edits, eval-as-tests).

resources.title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
resources.author: Yang et al.
resources.why: The clearest paper-treatment of "design the agent-computer interface, not the agent." The framing transfers across every coding-agent product.

nodes.label: Research-agent recipes
nodes.summary: The OpenAI-DeepResearch / Anthropic Research / Gemini Deep Research class: browse → read → cite → summarize loops, with careful citation and abstention discipline.
nodes.competent_means: Can describe the research-agent harness (search tool, reader/extractor, citation tracker, abstention rule) and the eval that catches fabricated citations.

resources.title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
resources.author: Asai et al.
resources.why: Cited here as adjacent rather than central — the "abstain when you can't ground" mechanism is what research agents import. The browse→read→cite loop itself doesn't yet have a canonical paper; revisit when one lands.
